text:
University College Hospital 

National Hospital for Neurology and Neurosurgery 

Clinic Date: 24 January 2015 

Dr Alison Green  
Riverside Medical Practice  
14 Wren Street, London WC1X 0BP  

Dear Dr Alison Green,  

Daniel Carter, DOB: 22-08-1978, Hospital No: C784210 NHS No. 3015529476  
Flat 3, 29 Brookfield Road, London E9 5AL 

Summary/ Diagnoses: History of seizures of uncertain classification; type 2 diabetes managed in primary care; dyspepsia; intermittent low mood under GP review; ex-smoker. 

Medication: 
- Levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. 
- Carbamazepine modified release 400 mg twice daily. 
- Metformin 1 g twice daily. 
- Omeprazole 20 mg once daily. 
- Sertraline 50 mg once daily. 

I reviewed him in clinic today accompanied by his mother, who provides most collateral history between appointments. He reports good general health and regular meals, and his mother confirms that medication adherence has been reliable with a weekly pill organiser. No recent intercurrent illnesses. He denies alcohol or recreational drugs. Sleep has been variable but improving with a consistent bedtime routine; he is engaging in short daily walks. Capillary glucose monitoring is performed in primary care; he did not bring a log today but recalls recent readings typically between 6–9 mmol/L fasting. He has not experienced hypoglycaemia symptoms. Weight is stable by report. 

Seizures: His mother notes an overall improvement compared with previous years, with four tonic seizures documented in 2015 so far. These have occurred without clear provocation according to the family diary. There is no reported prolonged postictal confusion, tongue biting, or injury this year. No witnessed episodes suggestive of status epilepticus. He is keeping a simple calendar-based record at home which I have encouraged them to continue bringing to clinic. 

Examination: He was alert and oriented with fluent speech. Cranial nerves intact. Motor power full with normal tone and coordination; no lateralising signs. Gait normal. Cardiovascular and respiratory examinations unremarkable. No peripheral stigmata of vascular disease. 

Diabetes: Managed by the GP team; no neuropathic symptoms volunteered and foot inspection today showed intact skin and normal capillary refill. I have reiterated the importance of annual retinal screening and routine HbA1c monitoring in primary care. 

Plan: 
- Continue current anti-seizure medication regimen without change. 
- Reinforce seizure diary completion and bring copies to next review. 
- Safety: advised standard precautions including supervised bathing and avoiding heights; driving not applicable as he does not hold a licence. 
- Liaise with practice to ensure HbA1c and lipids are up to date; defer diabetes medication management to primary care. 
- Arrange follow-up in neurology clinic in six months, or earlier if there is any increase in seizure frequency, new injury, or prolonged events. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: four tonic seizures documented in 2015 so far
His mother notes an overall improvement compared with previous years, with four tonic seizures documented in 2015 so far

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter states: "four tonic seizures documented in 2015 so far." The clinic date is 24 January 2015, which implies these 4 seizures occurred within January 2015 up to the clinic date. Therefore, the current frequency can be inferred as 4 in the current month. No other frequency data is provided. Hence, normalize to '4 per month'.
seizure_frequency_number: four tonic seizures documented in 2015 so far
reference: His mother notes an overall improvement compared with previous years, with four tonic seizures documented in 2015 so far
